item.dc:creator: LeeeB

item.title: Saving the memory
item.description:
Alberto "Alex" Errera (b.1913) was a former Captain in the Hellenic Army, he managed a supermarket when WW2 began; after the Nazi invasion of Greece he became a member of the Greek resistance group the Greek People's Liberation Army (ELAS). When he was captured in an anti-Partisan operation, he was sent to Auschwitz as prisoner number 182,552. Strong and healthy, he was selected for the Sonderkommando - a special category of slave labourer in the Nazi camps. Sonderkommando were specially selected healthy prisoners who had the task of dragging bodies from the gas chambers, and stripping the corpses of gold fillings, false teeth, hair, jewellery and spectacles. As the process of Zyklon B killing a sealed room of people could take up to an hour, the Sonderkommando were also the primary witnesses of the grisly evidence of the victim's last moments, such as ripped away fingernails and fingers broken by clawing at and pounding at the doors and walls, and children's skulls crushed by the weight of adult bodies falling atop them, and that the corpses were often "piled" inside the chamber, as the last survivors used their remaining moments to crawl desperately upwards on the bodies of others to reach hoped-for air higher in the chamber. In Auschwitz Sonderkommando then burned the bodies in pits & the crematoria. In Dachau, they operated "the ovens". In Janowska camp Sonderkommando operated a mill designed to crush bones to dust.
[Picture: Survivors of Janowska camp Sonderkommando (l-t-r, unknown, David Manusevitz, Moses Korn) stand next to the 'bone crushing machine' they operated, which is today in the National Museum of the History of Ukraine in the Second World War.] In August 1944, members of Auschwitz Sonderkommando V, in coordination with the Polish resistance, succeeded in documenting the otherwise hidden horror of the Nazi extermination process in four covertly taken pictures. Together with some photographs from the 'Auschwitz album', taken two months earlier, they form the only photographic record of events immediately around the Auschwitz gas chambers, and the only two images in existence of the gas chamber's body-destruction process in action. These images, by their existence, defy the Nazi state's deliberate effort to cover up its crimes - called Sonderacktion 1005 - and tell us a great deal about the agency & bravery of the prisoners in camps, particularly the tragic figures of the Sonderkommando. In this article, I can only use two of the images captured by Sonderkommando V, as the others are considered too distressing for Imgur's community standards. The images fall into two groups, two images taken from inside the prisoner undressing room, showing the burning of gassed corpses in a pit outside, and two of prisoners awaiting their murder in a nearby grove of trees - one of which is angled so that it only catches a view of the sky. They are known as 280, 281, 282 and 283, after their record numbers in the modern Auschwitz-Birkenau Museum. Sonderkommando were selected for the task without consultation, kept in their own barracks, fed more to remain physically capable and allowed luxuries (such as retaining cigarettes from arrival's belongings). They were supposed to be killed en masse every three months to maintain the security & secrecy of the extermination process but some who had special skills - such as those who learnt by bitter experience precisely how long it took to immolate a body to ash - survived for up to a year. It is estimated that there were 14 "generations" of camp Sonderkommandos, with the first task of new Sonderkommando being the disposal of the bodies of their predecessors.
[Picture: One of the four pictures I can use here, taken from the hip, this shows women being driven from amongst the trees -heading towards the gas chamber; and so it captures the very last moments of life these people would have.] The Sonderkommando photographer is named by witnesses as "Alex" and has been reasonably strongly, but not conclusively identified as Alberto Errera - Sonderkommando had little reason to talk about their lives & families. "Alex" died a week or two later, he was sent outside the camp perimeter to discharge the ash of burnt prisoners into the Vistula river, on arrival, he stunned two guards with a shovel before fleeing into the river. He was recaptured two or three days later, executed after torture, and his body hung up in the camp. Other members of the Sonderkommando involved have been identified as Alter Fajnzylberg (also known as Stanisław Jankowski), brothers Shlomo and Josel Dragon, and David Szmulewski. Although "Alex" took the pictures, this was very much a group operation, due to the necessity of group cooperation to hide the camera and provide look-outs during the taking of the pictures. The camera was probably a small German Leica camera, smuggled into the camp in a bucket. The film from the camera was hidden inside a tube of toothpaste by Helena Dantón, who worked in the SS canteen, and then smuggled outside the camp to the Polish resistance.
[Picture: One of the "dressing room" door images was clearly the inspiration for this panel in "Maus" (1980-1991, collected 1991) in the pose of the Sonderkommando gingerly stepping through a pile of bodies, which is a mirrored pose from Sonderkommando picture today known as "280". ] Fajnzylberg believed that all the pictures were taken within 10-30 minutes of each other, however analysis of the photographs has produced several differing theories on the timescale documented. Some believe, judging from shadow positioning, that the photographs of naked people in the trees were taken in the morning, while the burning pictures were taken in the afternoon, potentially documenting the arrival, and disposal of bodies, of one distinct group. When the pictures were published and circulated by the Polish resistance and Polish-Government-in-Exile, the pictures taken from inside the 'dressing room' (280, 281) were cropped to take away the framing doorway, which some believe lessened the impact of the pictures, by making them seem to have been taken "safely" instead of covertly; 'erasing' the brave act of severely risky resistance the photographs represent.
[Picture: The sky and some trees, evidence of the covert way the pictures had to be taken. It is assumed this was meant to be a further shot of the nude women in the tress but was angled too sharply.] The Nazi regime launched Sonderaktion 1005 in March 1942, beginning a phased cover-up operation of Nazi crimes, beginning with the exhumation and destruction of mass burials left by early "ad hoc" mass executions by Einsatzgruppen (Special Teams) of German forces before the beginning of the industrialised death camp process. Leichenkommandos - "corpse teams" - of prisoners were organised in the concentration camps to likewise exhume unburnt bodies from earlier phases and incinerate them. If the Nazis had not lost the war, it is probable that the regime would have found further ways to memocide any knowledge of their crimes. It is not often recalled that the Nazi decision to move to industrialise mass-murder by gassing was partially caused by the mental health toll mass executions placed on German troops, with discipline issues and alcoholism spiralling in the Einsatzgruppen, a more efficient, "distant" way to mass murder was needed. Forcing prisoners to do a significant proportion of the 'dirty work' themselves reduced the mental burden on German troops. The comparative cheapness of using a pre-existing pesticide (Zyklon B) in a few centralised locations compared to the use of "gas-trucks" (sealed trucks with the exhaust discharged into the truck) or bullets fired by roving military units (with the necessary attendant support units & logistical demands) was also a contributing factor. It's also important to note that most of these methods were originally developed as part of the Nazi euthanasia program that targeted the mentally ill of their own nation, and was scaled up from there.
[Picture: from the Auschwitz album, a group mainly made up of older men and women, and very small children, all judged incapable of productive labour; within minutes of death in the gas chambers.] Sonderkommando survivors of the Holocaust, due to their intimate involvement in the process of murder, were often socially isolated from other survivors, being regarded as on a similar level to the collaborationist "Kapo" prisoner-overseers (noted for their brutality). Art Spiegelman, author of the magisterial "Maus" (1991), overheard his father explaining at one survivors event why no one would sit near a certain man, as he was a Sonderkommando - 'he'd put his own son in the ovens'. However, Sonderkommando testimonies, such as that of Zalman Gradowski, who also recorded the names of his already-murdered family, of the death camp process have been found buried in Auschwitz, desperate attempts to record the Nazi crimes by those forced to be part of them. The only known organised prisoner rebellion in Auschwitz took place in October 1944 when Sonderkommando IV successfully killed three SS guards as part of an abortive plan to blow up one of the gas chambers. Some hundreds of Sonderkommando prisoners were executed for this rebellion - including Zalman Gradowski, who noted in his buried record his hope: "May the world at least behold a drop, a fraction of this tragic world in which we lived."
[Picture: More victims from the Auschwitz Album, Hungarian Jews from Carpatho-Ruthenia (today part of Ukraine), most were moved to Auschwitz from the Berehovo Ghetto, which was used as a collection area for Jews from many parts of Hungary.] Out of several thousand prisoners who worked as Sonderkommandos, only about 20 are known to have survived until liberation (though unwillingness to be identifed as a Sonderkommando may have reduced this number). Modern readings on the actions of Sonderkommando are generally softer than in the immediate post-war period. The only ways to stop being a Sonderkommando was execution by the Nazis, or suicide. There are suggestions that some Sonderkommando committed suicide by voluntarily joining groups in the gas chambers, out of despair, guilt, or both. SS-Standartenführer Paul Blobel (1894-1951), who amongst a whole litany of crimes against humanity, including the Babi Yar mass-murder and pioneering the use of sealed "gas vans" to mass-murder, was tasked with leading the memocide operation Sonderaktion 1005. He was executed following trial in 1951. He attempted to 'correct the record' during his testimony, declaring that he was not personally responsible for 60,000 murders, as the prosecution said, but "only" 10,000-15,000. The actions of Sonderkommando IV, in the face of unrelenting horror and brutality, remind us all that the most important credo of defending human dignity comes in two parts - Never Forget - by not allowing memory so hard-won to erode in detail & clarity, and - Never Again. [The Sonderkommando photographs, https://www.jewishvirtuallibrary.org/sonderkommando-photographs-from-auschwitz ] [The Auschwitz album, the only other photographic record which takes us close to the gas chambers, likely taken between May-June 1944 by SS officer Bernhard Walter, head of the Auschwitz photographic unit, for reasons unknown: https://www.yadvashem.org/yv/en/exhibitions/album_auschwitz/index.asp ]

item.title: Day 255: of cheeseburgers I want inside of me

item.title: There are good folks in Tennessee
item.description:
Not everyone in Tennessee is an ignorant book burner.
Our state is massively effed up, but there are good people.
I’m so often ashamed of Tennessee so it’s great to see actual good news. “The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good people to do nothing.” Good people have plenty to keep them busy in Tennessee.
https://gofund.me/f595c058

item.title: Wait for the flash
item.description:
Czesława Kwoka was born on 15 August 1928; this picture was taken in late 1942. A Polish Catholic, her and her mother Katarzyna were transported to the Auschwitz-Birkenau complex following Aktion Zamosc, an attempt by Nazi Germany to ethnically cleanse the Polish region of Zamosc in order to facilitate later German colonisation. One aspect of the Nazi myth of German victimhood that powered and justified the Nazi worldview alleged that German families, and in particular children, who lived in Poland following the post-World War One re-creation of that state were forcibly Polonised as part of a campaign of German cultural erasure. To "counteract" this, Nazi Germany operated a vast program of child abduction across occupied Poland - about 30,000 is the usual estimate. Children considered "Germanic"-seeming enough were passed to adoption agencies in Germany. The process of a child being 'confirmed' in their Germanness inevitably resulted in children who, 'on closer inspection' were not found German enough for quack Nazi eugenicists.
[Picture: Rollcall in Kinder KZ Lodz.] This led to the creation of Kinder KZ Lindmannstadt - Children's Concentration Camp Lodz - a fenced-off part of the Lodz Jewish Ghetto for these "not German enough" children, where they became part of the forced labour force of the Ghetto. Polish children caught engaging in petty crimes, or found to be homeless, potentially due to the arrest or execution of their parents or simply found without adult supervision during 'sweeps' by Nazi forces - were also confined in the Kinder KZ; there exist excruciating letters from children in the camp to their parents and carers. 20,000 children would be confined there in the Nazi state's only child-only concentration camp. A sub-camp existed outside the city at Dzierżązna, filled only with girls who produced food for the city camp. SS Aufseherin Sydomia Bayer (1903-executed 1945) killed at least one little girl at the camp, 13-year-old Urszula Kaczmarek, by either flogging her to death, or allowing her to be left in the snow outside the camp's building until she succumbed to the elements. It is probable that these conflicting records represent either two incidents in the abuse of one girl, or two separate murders of which one remains unidentified.
[Picture: Elzbieta Konarska, confined in Lodz Children's Concentration Camp on 14 September 1943. Her fate is unknown.] Czesława Kwoka was evidently not considered 'Germanic' enough for abduction, nor considered young enough for sending to Lodz, and so was condemned to immediate removal to Auschwitz-Birkenau. She died seven months after arrival at the camp on 12 March 1943, aged 14. There are conflicting reports of Czesława's death, it is possible she was killed by an experimental injection of phenol (a disinfectant) into her heart in one of the first human experiments overseen by newly-arrived Doctor Josef Mengele (1911-1979). The photographer who took her picture recalled that Czesława was hit across the face with a truncheon shortly before the picture was taken, hence her split lip.
[Picture: An unknown Dutch Jehovah's Witness, inmate number 25 563] Her picture was taken by Wilhelm Brasse (1917-2012), a Polish man of German ethnicity who had been a photographer before the war and spoke German; having refused to cooperate with the new German authority's attempts to racially classify him he was arrested and deported to Auschwitz. He was consequently forced into the Politische Abteilung Erkennungsdienst ("Political Department Identification Service") of Auschwitz - the photography unit which took "mugshots" of arrivals into Auschwitz, as well as general documenting of camp operations. Concentration camps operated nominally as if they were prisons, as they were before some were tasked primarily with murder. Consequently, bureaucratic procedural inertia meant they kept detailed records of inmates for the purpose of maximising productivity and security, for example if an inmate escaped, their mugshot could be circulated. (There were 928 known escapes from Auschwitz, including Konstanty Jagiełło (1916-1944), who was killed during a subsequent Polish resistance attempt to release more prisoners, and one prisoner uprising by the Crematoria IV Sonderkommando.)
[Picture: Still image from a Soviet film of survivors of Auschwitz following liberation by the Red Army; this picture includes Tomasz Szwarz; Alicja Gruenbaum; Solomon Rozalin; Gita Sztrauss; Wiera Sadler; Marta Wiess; Boro Eksztein; Josef Rozenwaser; Rafael Szlezinger; Gabriel Nejman; Adek Apfelbaum; Hillik (later Harold) Apfelbaum; Mark Berkowitz (a twin); Pesa Balter; Rut Muszkies (later Webber); Miriam Friedman; and twins Miriam Mozes & Eva Mozes wearing knitted hats. Twins were a particular area of interest in human experimentation at Auschwitz.] The Auschtwitz photography unit was led by SS-Hauptscharführer Bernhard Walter (1911-1979) and deputy director SS-Unterscharführer Ernst Hofmann (1901-?); its manpower was mainly made up of prisoners, led by Franz Maltz, a German inmate. They each existed in a tenuous manner, kept alive for their skills, but functionally unable to act in any independent way for fear of their lives. Prisoners forced to used their skills to document the camp's operations besides Wilhelm Brasse (prisoner number 3444) include Tadeusz Brodka (245), Eugeniusz Dembek (63764), Edward Josefsberg, Bronisław Jureczek (26672), Roman Karwat (5959), Leonid Koren (21953), Tadeusz Krzysica (120557), Tadeusz Myszkowski (593), Zdzisław Pazio (3078), Jozef Pysz (1420), Jozef Swiatloch (3529), Stanisław Tralka (660), Wladyslaw Wawrzyniak (9449), and Alfred Woycicki (39247).
[Picture: Aron Lwi, born 15 April 1879, a Jewish merchant from Zakow - he arrived in Auschwitz on 5 March 1942 as prisoner 26406. He died in the camp 5 days later, at the age of 62. ] Hauptscharfuhrer Walter himself took several photographs in an album now known as the "Auschwitz Album", which was seemingly an attempt to create a kind of "day in the life of Auschwitz" pictoral history of the Auschwitz extermination process when it was dealing with an unusually large number of arrivals after the mass deportation of Hungarian Jews from May-July 1944, when over 400,000 people were sent to Auschwitz, prompting the Nazis to 'steamline' the camp's induction process even further to cope with the numbers arriving. Consisting of around 200 photographs, of which 193 survive, the album shows every aspect of camp induction operations, from the arrival of predominantly Jews by train, the robbery of their property, and their division into slave labourers and those to be immediately killed.
[Picture: Peter Siedielnikow; inmate number 84893] The Auschwitz Album was discovered in a cupboard inside the SS barracks at Dora Concentration Camp - 400 miles away from Auschwitz - by Lili Jacob (1926-1999), a former inmate of Auschwitz, while the former barracks was being used as a hospital following liberation. A great many of the most well-known images of the Holocaust are from this album. The sequence of events that led to this unique record of Nazi criminality surviving are unknown.
[Picture: Janina Olszynska, prisoner number 8558; born August 12 1892, her, her husband, and son were arrested for being part of the Polish Resistance and sent to Auschwitz on 11 July 1942. Janina and her son Jan both survived Auschwitz; her husband Bolesław did not.] At Auschwitz, as the Red Army approached, prisoner-photographers Walter Brasse and Bronisław Jureczek of the photography unit were ordered to burn the images of prisoners and other records. By careful packing of the stove they were using for this, ensuring it'd starve of oxygen and go out, and scattering vast amounts of photographs and negatives to create too big a job to tidy up, they allowed 38,000 photographs to survive and be collected once the Red army liberated the camp. Wilhelm Brasse struggled to continue with photography after World War Two. His memories and testimony were crucial in several post-war trials of Nazis. He and the other surviving inmates of the photography unit had the unique distinction of being victims of the Holocaust, witnesses of the Holocaust, recorders of the Holocaust, and some of the earliest to grasp the full scale of what the Final Solution was, as they processed and developed records of crimes they'd not themselves been witness to. You can view the whole Auschwitz album here: https://www.yadvashem.org/yv/en/exhibitions/album_auschwitz/index.asp The photo of Czesława Kwoka, originally black and white in the archive of Memorial Oświęcim, was coloured by Mirek Szponar, and inspired this post. http://www.mirekszponar.pl/
item.dc:creator: BodicaRexVII

item.title: Indid a thing
item.description: Yepp. I know its really late but I finally worked past my fear of what could happen and got my first vaccine today. Also got my flu shot. So far the flu shots kicking in and making everything sore and Im nervous about how ill feel tomorrow from the covid vaccine but hey at least I got it. Most viral edit: wow, this blew up. I woke up in the middle of the night and seen all of this! Ill sit down and reply to folks tomorrow when im fully awake but I wanted to throw a few things out there while im thinking of them. First, yeah. I get it. Im the asshole for waiting so long. I have anxiety issues and people close to me that were worried about long term side effects of the vaccine. So yeah, I dragged my feet. Second, Im not anti vaxx. Im terrified of covid so I never go anywhere without masks, I use way too much sanitizer, and leave my house MAYBE 3 times a month for groceries or dr appointments. Im not out rampantly spreading anything. Third, either im one of the asymptomatic folks or Ive managed to not get covid yet. So there's that. And last, I have an appointment made for the second shot. Kind of pointless to get just one, eh? To all of you being supportive, thank you. It means a lot and warms my heart. The advice helps me a lot, too. You guys are awesome.
item.dc:creator: Hidatsu

item.title: Joe Rogan 2 years ago… anybody wanna guess why he changed that viewpoint?
item.description:
Here is the full interview so you can see that this is NOT out of context… enjoy. https://youtu.be/E3URhJx0NSw
“So many wackos out there that think that vaccines are a scam or are dangerous. So many people that won’t vaccinate their children.” - Joe Rogan

item.title: Pls help me locate my lost stuffed bear
item.description:
TL;DR: Someone took my large, purple Samonsite upright checked bag from Carousel 10 at JFK (from flight DL0324) on Sat, Feb 5 at around 3-3:30pm et. It has irreplaceable stuffed animals my recently late father gave to me when I was a kiddo; if you have this please call Delta and they’ll pay to get it back to me. I’ll also pay you something; i’m by no means rich, but if money is what’s keeping you, i’d try to make something work. Hi! I know this is only my second post and I’ve been quite the bummer, but I wanted to reach out in hopes that this would find the right people. On Saturday, February 5, I took Delta flight 0324 from LAX to JFK to move to NYC for a new job and someone mistakenly (I hope) took my checked bag. (We landed around 2:50 pm et, and at 3:30pm et the checked bags started coming down carousel 10.)
It’s a large (maybe 29in) purple samsonite upright fabric bag. Here’s a potato photo of it.
And here’s a photo of my mom’s suitcase—it’s the same build but a different color (mine is purple).
I don’t really care that much about most of the contents of the bag (I mean I do, it’s my clothes and books and what little I own) but the things I care most that I’ve lost right now are some stuffed animals my late father had gifted me over the years. My dad died of kidney cancer about 7 months ago and I miss him terribly. So these toys are priceless and absolutely irreplaceable to me, especially as I move away from home and grieve.
Here’s some photos of them
And here’s a photo of me and my dad. I was his primary caretaker when he was sick, and i miss him everyday. If you can, can you please share this. I’m hoping this will reach people who are in NY or you know were on that flight. I’d do anything to get those sentimental items back; i know they’re not much, but they’re priceless to me. You can DM if you have any information. If cash is what’s keeping you from calling delta and returning it, i’ll pay you! I don’t have much, but i’d make it work to get them back. Also, I packed in such a rush/haze, I’m not sure if I put my copy of my passport in it to ID me. I can tell you what is in the most front facing compartment and what you see when you first open it to confirm though (as well as info on other contents). First post for reference : https://imgur.com/gallery/QZfD9Jq
Pup tax
item.dc:creator: waitingfortickets

item.title: Don't help him, he's so close!
item.description: Exactly Kevin. Why aren't Insulin, Chemotherapy, and Epipens cheaper and more cost effective for the American citizens? Watching his breakdown on Twitter today has been fun. https://twitter.com/ksorbs/status/1490773752841396230 https://twitter.com/susieq2457/status/1490839094137692160
item.dc:creator: Imadethisaccounttopost
